agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature where customers can view their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have presented directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, while Operations demands no location sharing to protect driver retention.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver/delivery person fulfilling the last-mile delivery to the customer."},{"term":"Real-time Location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates as reported by their device during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to the customer with their order."},{"term":"Location Visibility","definition":"The degree to which a courier's position is exposed to the end customer — ranges from exact GPS coordinates to no visibility at all."},{"term":"Driver Retention","definition":"The operational metric measuring how many couriers remain active on the platform over time. Directly affected by working conditions and perceived surveillance."},{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — a derived value communicating when the delivery will occur, which may or may not require exposing raw location data."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., traffic, rest stop) — exact GPS makes customer anxious and courier feel surveilled simultaneously.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until contradiction is resolved. This scenario amplifies the tension between both positions."},{"scenario":"Courier deviates from expected route (multi-drop, personal errand) — exact GPS reveals behavior customer may misinterpret.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until contradiction is resolved. Exact GPS creates both a customer concern and a driver privacy violation."},{"scenario":"Courier is in a sensitive location (hospital, residential area not related to delivery).","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. Raises additional privacy/safety concerns that strengthen Operations' position."},{"scenario":"Customer claims non-delivery but courier GPS shows they were at location.","expected_behavior":"Internal GPS audit trail (not customer-facing) may satisfy this need without exposing real-time location. This is a potential middle-ground data point."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — This is a two-sided marketplace problem. Losing customers (no transparency) reduces revenue. Losing couriers (surveillance concerns) collapses supply, which is typically harder and more expensive to rebuild than demand. Driver acquisition costs in logistics typically range 5-10x the cost of customer acquisition marketing. A supply-side collapse is an existential threat; a transparency gap is a competitive disadvantage.","operational":"CRITICAL — Operations has reported measurable driver attrition directly attributed to location surveillance. This is not a hypothetical risk; it is an active retention crisis. Any feature that worsens driver churn will degrade delivery SLAs, increase costs, and create a negative spiral.","user":"Both sides represent real user needs. Customers want predictability and control over their time. Couriers want dignity and autonomy in their work. These are legitimate, but they are not equally served by 'exact GPS coordinates at all times.'","risk":"Shipping either requirement as stated carries severe risk. Full GPS transparency risks regulatory exposure under GDPR/privacy laws (courier location is personal data), driver attrition, and potential labor disputes. Zero visibility risks customer churn and competitive disadvantage in a market where tracking is table stakes.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery predictability. The actual need is likely ETA confidence and delivery status, not raw GPS surveillance of a human being."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected. Exact GPS sharing to customers constitutes workplace surveillance. Attrition is already occurring. This is a safety and dignity issue, not just preference."},{"group":"Customer Success Team","impact":"Advocating for customer transparency as a differentiator. Their goal is valid but their proposed solution (exact GPS) is one of many ways to achieve it."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Advocating for driver welfare and retention. Their blanket 'never show location' may overcorrect, but their underlying concern is existentially important."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED but must be. Sharing a worker's real-time GPS coordinates with third parties has significant privacy law implications (GDPR Article 6, various US state privacy laws, labor regulations)."},{"group":"Product/Executive Leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. This is a business strategy decision, not a feature decision. It defines the company's position on its marketplace balance."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because two authoritative internal stakeholders have issued directly contradictory requirements, both framed as non-negotiable. No spec can be correct when the inputs contradict. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not weighed in, and this feature has significant privacy law implications that neither team has addressed. The actual business need (customer predictability vs. driver dignity) likely has a middle-ground solution, but that solution has not been sanctioned by either party or by leadership.","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine stakeholder contradiction that cannot be resolved by engineering","Driver retention is a more existential risk than customer transparency gaps","The customer's real need is likely ETA confidence, not raw GPS coordinates","Legal/privacy review is mandatory before any location-sharing feature ships","Industry standard (Uber, DoorDash, Amazon) uses abstracted location (map pin on a route, ETA countdown) rather than exact GPS"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether leadership will side with Customer Success, Operations, or a compromise","Specific privacy regulations applicable to this company's jurisdiction","Whether the driver attrition data is causal or correlational","Customer churn data attributable to lack of tracking"],"assumptions":["Both teams have authority to raise requirements but neither has unilateral authority to override the other","No executive decision has already been made that resolves this","The company operates in jurisdictions where worker location privacy is legally relevant","The courier workforce is contracted or employed (not autonomous robots)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS visibility to customers at all times. Operations requires zero location visibility to customers, ever. These are mutually exclusive requirements from peer-authority teams. Neither can be implemented without violating the other. This is NOT an engineering problem — it is a business strategy decision about marketplace balance (demand-side transparency vs. supply-side welfare) that must be resolved by executive leadership.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success","Operations","Legal/Compliance","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Full GPS transparency (Customer Success wins, Operations loses — expect accelerated driver attrition)","B: Zero location visibility (Operations wins, Customer Success loses — competitive disadvantage vs. market)","C: Abstracted location — show ETA, delivery status, and approximate proximity (e.g., '5 minutes away') without exposing exact GPS coordinates. This is the industry-standard compromise used by Uber, DoorDash, and Amazon. Satisfies customer predictability need without surveilling drivers.","D: Configurable per-courier — let drivers opt into location sharing with informed consent, show ETA-only for those who opt out. Respects driver autonomy while allowing transparency for willing participants."],"recommendation":"Option C (Abstracted location with ETA). This is the dominant industry pattern because it resolves the exact tension described here. Customers get predictability and a sense of control. Drivers are not surveilled. Legal risk is minimized. Customer Success gets a differentiator (great ETA accuracy, proactive status updates) without the liability. HOWEVER — I cannot set acceptance criteria or business rules until leadership formally resolves this contradiction. I am escalating this to Coach K and recommending an executive-level decision forum with both teams, Legal, and Product leadership present."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot accept Customer Success requirement as stated","business_rule_broken":"Sharing a worker's exact GPS coordinates with third-party customers without consent likely violates privacy regulations and is demonstrably causing driver attrition — an existential supply-side risk."},{"violation":"Cannot accept Operations requirement as stated","business_rule_broken":"Zero location visibility in a logistics product ignores the market-standard customer expectation of delivery tracking. This is a competitive necessity, not a nice-to-have. The requirement overcorrects by conflating 'exact GPS' with 'any location signal.'"},{"violation":"Both requirements are presented as non-negotiable but are mutually exclusive","business_rule_broken":"No valid system specification can satisfy both constraints simultaneously. Proceeding with either requirement as-is would be domain-incorrect. This must be resolved at the business level before engineering begins."}]}
